1: Psalm 23 is the most universally known of all the Psalms. The shepherd metaphor was a standard royal image across the ancient Near East: Hammurabi of Babylon called himself 'the shepherd of his people'; Egyptian pharaohs were depicted with the shepherd's crook and flail; Sumerian king Ur-Nammu was 'the faithful shepherd.' David was literally a shepherd before becoming king (1 Sam 16:11; 17:34-36), which gave the metaphor biographical as well as political depth. YHWH as Israel's shepherd appears in Ps 80:1 ('O Shepherd of Israel, hear us'), Ezek 34 (YHWH vs. the false shepherds), and Isa 40:11.
2: 'He makes me lie down in green pastures; he leads me beside quiet waters' — pastoral geography of the Judean hill country. Green pastures (niot deshe) were seasonal in the dry climate; after the spring rains, the hillsides turned green briefly before summer drought. Still (menukhot, resting) waters rather than rushing streams: sheep will not drink from fast-moving water and can drown in flash floods in the Judean wadis. The shepherd's knowledge of terrain — where to find safe water and productive grazing — was specialized expertise that determined the flock's survival.
3: 'He restores my inner life (nephesh); he guides me along right paths for the sake of his name' — the shepherd leads the sheep along known, safe paths (magelei-tsedek, tracks of righteousness) that reach productive grazing without danger. The metaphor carries moral weight: the 'right paths' are the covenant way. 'For the sake of his name' — YHWH's reputation as faithful shepherd-patron is at stake in whether he guides his people well. The shepherd's honor is bound to the flock's condition.
4: 'Even though I walk through the valley of deepest darkness (ge tsalmavet), I will fear no evil, for you are with me' — the shadowed ravines of the Judean hills where predators (lions, bears, wolves) lurked and where flash floods could trap flocks. Shepherds regularly led sheep through these valleys to reach highland pastures. 'Your rod and your staff, they comfort me' — the rod (shevet) was used to drive off predators (David describes using a staff against lion and bear in 1 Sam 17:35); the staff (mishenet) guided and recovered straying sheep from dangerous terrain. Both were tools of active protection, not passive presence.
5: 'You set a table for me in full view of my enemies; you anoint my head with oil; my cup overflows' — the scene shifts from pastoral to royal banquet. The prepared table in the presence of enemies is the paradox of covenant abundance within threat. Shepherds literally anointed the heads of sheep with oil to repel insects in summer heat and to heal wounds from thorns; the practice was routine pastoral care. But the anointing here evokes royal anointing (1 Sam 10:1; 16:13) and priestly hospitality. The cup overflowing (revayah) is the abundance that exceeds what is needed.
6: 'Surely goodness and steadfast love (hesed) will pursue me all the days of my life, and I will live in the house of the LORD forever' — the reversal of the 'pursue' language that fills the lament psalms: enemies pursue the righteous (Ps 7:1; 109:16; 143:3); here it is YHWH's goodness and hesed that pursue. The committed covenant-loyalty of YHWH is not merely available but actively tracking the psalmist. 'The house of the LORD forever' — dwelling in YHWH's presence permanently; for the Psalm's original audience, the temple; in its fullest reach, eschatological presence.
